9.text: Standard library only — no NumPy, no PyTorch, no nothing. This is pure Python all the way down.

10.text: Just log and exp — the two math functions that make neural networks work.

11.text: Random numbers for weight initialization, sampling during generation, and shuffling data.

12.text: Fixed seed so every run is identical — essential for debugging when something goes wrong.

15.text: Builds the path to the dataset file relative to this script, one directory up in data/.

16.text: Auto-downloads the dataset if missing — the tutorial is self-contained.

17.text: Lazy import: only load urllib if we actually need to download.

18.text: Karpathy's "makemore" names dataset — ~32K human names, perfect for a tiny language model.

19.text: One HTTP call and we have our training data. No pip install, no API key.

20.text: Each document is one name. The model will learn the statistical patterns of how names are spelled.

21.text: Shuffling prevents the model from memorizing the alphabetical order of the dataset.

25.text: The vocabulary is just the unique characters across all names — typically a-z. Each character gets an integer ID.

26.text: A special token marking where names begin and end. The model learns to generate BOS when a name is "done."

27.text: Total vocabulary: 26 letters + 1 BOS token = 27 symbols. That's the entire language.

31.text: This is the heart of the autograd engine. Every Value wraps a single scalar number and knows how to compute its own gradient.

32.text: __slots__ saves memory by preventing Python from creating a __dict__ per instance — matters when you have thousands of Values.

34.text: Each Value stores four things: the number itself, its gradient, what produced it, and the local derivatives connecting it to its children.

35.text: The forward pass fills data; the backward pass fills grad.

36.text: Starts at zero because gradients accumulate — multiple paths through the graph can contribute to the same parameter's gradient.

37.text: The computation graph is built implicitly: every operation creates a new Value that remembers its inputs.

38.text: Local gradients are the partial derivatives of this node with respect to each child — the chain rule's building blocks.

40.text: Operator overloading is the trick that makes autograd invisible. Writing a + b secretly builds the computation graph.

41.text: If you add a plain number to a Value, wrap it first. This lets you write x + 1 naturally.

42.text: Addition: result is a + b, and both local gradients are 1 because d(a+b)/da = d(a+b)/db = 1.

44.text: Multiplication builds the graph and stores local gradients. The derivative of a*b w.r.t. a is b, and vice versa — the "swap and multiply" rule of calculus.

46.text: Product rule in action: d(a*b)/da = b and d(a*b)/db = a. The child values become each other's local gradients.

48.text: Power rule: d(x^n)/dx = n*x^(n-1). Used for division (x^-1) and square roots (x^0.5).

49.text: d(log x)/dx = 1/x. This appears in the cross-entropy loss computation.

50.text: d(exp x)/dx = exp(x) — the only function that is its own derivative. Beautiful and essential for softmax.

51.text: ReLU: pass positive values through, kill negatives. The gradient is 1 or 0 — a simple gate.

52.text: Negation reuses multiplication: -x is just x * -1. No new backward logic needed.

53.text: Right-add: handles 3 + value by flipping to value + 3. Python calls this when the left operand doesn't know how to add.

57.text: Division is multiplication by the reciprocal: a/b = a * b^(-1). The power rule handles the rest.

60.text: Backpropagation: walk the computation graph in reverse topological order, applying the chain rule at each node.

61.text: Topological sort ensures we process each node only after all nodes that depend on it. Gradients flow backward through the graph.

63.text: Recursive depth-first traversal to build the topological ordering — the classic algorithm.

69.text: Start the sort from the loss node — it's the root of the computation graph.

70.text: The loss's gradient w.r.t. itself is 1. This is the seed that starts the chain reaction of gradient computation.

71.text: Reversed topological order means we visit the loss first, then work backward toward the parameters. Each node distributes its gradient to its children.

72.text: For each node, multiply the incoming gradient by each local gradient and add to the child's gradient. This IS the chain rule.

73.text: The += is crucial — a parameter used in multiple operations receives gradients from all of them. This is why grad starts at 0.

76.text: 16 dimensions per token embedding. Tiny by modern standards (GPT-3 uses 12,288), but enough to learn name patterns.

77.text: 4 attention heads, each seeing a 4-dimensional slice. Multiple heads let the model attend to different types of relationships simultaneously.

78.text: Just 1 transformer layer. Real GPTs stack 12-96+ layers, but one layer already captures interesting patterns.

79.text: Names longer than 16 characters get truncated. Most names fit easily.

80.text: Each head gets n_embd / n_head = 4 dimensions. The heads work in parallel on different subspaces.

81.text: A helper to create a 2D list of random Values — this is how we initialize weight matrices without NumPy.

82.text: The three core weight matrices: token embeddings (what each character means), position embeddings (where it sits), and the output head (predicting the next character).

83.text: For each transformer layer, allocate the attention and MLP weight matrices.

84.text: Query weights: "what am I looking for?"

86.text: Value weights: "what information do I carry?"

87.text: Output projection: recombines the multi-head attention outputs back into a single vector.

88.text: MLP first layer expands to 4x the embedding dimension — a wider hidden layer for more expressive transformations.

89.text: MLP second layer projects back down to the embedding dimension.

90.text: Flatten all weight matrices into one list so the optimizer can iterate over every parameter uniformly.

95.text: A linear layer as a pure function: matrix-vector multiply using nested list comprehensions. No classes, no frameworks — just math.

96.text: Each output element is a dot product between one row of the weight matrix and the input vector. This is one row of Wx.

98.text: Softmax converts raw scores (logits) into a probability distribution that sums to 1.

99.text: Subtract the max for numerical stability — prevents exp() from overflowing. Doesn't change the output probabilities.

100.text: Exponentiate to make everything positive, then normalize. This is the standard softmax formula.

101.text: Sum of exponentials becomes the normalizing constant.

102.text: Each probability is its exponential divided by the total. Now they sum to 1 and represent a valid distribution.

104.text: RMSNorm: a simpler, faster alternative to LayerNorm. No mean subtraction, no learned parameters — just scale by the root mean square.

105.text: Mean of squared values — measures the "energy" of the vector.

106.text: Inverse square root scaling factor. The 1e-5 prevents division by zero when all values are near zero.

107.text: Scale each element so the vector has roughly unit RMS. This stabilizes training by preventing values from growing unboundedly.

109.text: The GPT forward pass. Takes one token and its position, plus KV caches for previous tokens. Returns prediction logits for the next token.

110.text: Look up this token's learned embedding — a dense vector capturing what the character "means" to the model.

111.text: Look up the position embedding — the model needs to know WHERE in the sequence this token sits.

112.text: Add token identity and position information. After this, the vector knows both what and where.

113.text: Normalize before entering the transformer layers — keeps activations stable from the start.

115.text: Process through each transformer layer. With n_layer=1, this loop runs once, but the code generalizes to deeper models.

117.text: Save the input for the residual connection — it will be added back after attention, creating a "skip road" for gradients.

118.text: Pre-norm architecture: normalize before attention, not after. This is the modern convention (GPT-2 onward) and helps training stability.

119.text: Query projection: transforms the normalized input into a "question" vector — what is this token looking for?

120.text: Key projection: transforms into a "label" vector — what does this token advertise about itself?

121.text: Value projection: transforms into the actual content that will be retrieved if attention deems this token relevant.

122.text: Append this token's keys to the cache. During generation, we accumulate keys from all previous positions so we don't recompute them.

123.text: Same for values — the KV cache is what makes autoregressive generation efficient.

125.text: Process each attention head independently. Each head can specialize in different types of token relationships.

126.text: Slice start index for this head's portion of the embedding.

127.text: Extract this head's slice of the query vector — just 4 dimensions out of 16.

128.text: Gather this head's key slices from ALL previous tokens (via the cache). This is the "memory" the current token attends to.

129.text: Same for values — the information to retrieve.

130.text: Scaled dot-product attention scores. Each score measures how much the query "matches" each key. Division by sqrt(head_dim) prevents the dot products from growing too large, which would make softmax too peaked.

131.text: Softmax converts attention scores into weights that sum to 1 — a probability distribution over which past tokens to attend to.

132.text: Weighted sum of value vectors, using attention weights. Tokens that "match" the query contribute more to the output. This is the core of the attention mechanism.

133.text: Concatenate all heads' outputs. Multi-head attention is just independent attentions run in parallel, then merged.

134.text: Output projection: mixes the concatenated head outputs. Lets the model learn how to combine information from different heads.

135.text: Residual connection: add the attention output to the original input. This creates a direct gradient highway and lets the model learn incremental refinements.

137.text: Save input for the MLP's residual connection.

139.text: MLP first layer: expand to 4x dimension. This wider hidden layer gives the network more capacity for complex transformations.

140.text: ReLU activation: zero out negatives, keep positives. Introduces non-linearity — without this, stacking linear layers would be equivalent to a single linear layer.

141.text: MLP second layer: project back to embedding dimension.

142.text: Second residual connection: add MLP output to its input. The pattern is always: normalize, transform, add back.

144.text: Final projection: map from the 16-dim embedding space to the 27-dim vocabulary. Each output is a score (logit) for how likely each character is to come next.

145.text: Return the logits — raw scores before softmax. The training loop will convert these to probabilities and compute the loss.

148.text: Adam optimizer hyperparameters. beta1=0.85 (momentum) is lower than the default 0.9 — less smoothing of gradients. beta2=0.99 tracks the scale of recent gradients.

149.text: First moment (mean) estimates — initialized to zero. These smooth out noisy gradients, acting like momentum.

150.text: Second moment (variance) estimates — these adapt the learning rate per-parameter, giving larger updates to rarely-changed parameters.

153.text: 1000 training steps. Each step processes one name and updates all parameters.

154.text: The main training loop — each iteration is one gradient descent step.

157.text: Cycle through the dataset. After seeing all names, start over.

158.text: Tokenize: BOS + character IDs + BOS. The model learns to start generating after BOS and stop by predicting BOS.

159.text: Clip to the maximum context length. Most names are short, so this rarely activates.

162.text: Fresh KV caches for each training example. During training, we process the whole sequence at once (teacher forcing).

163.text: We'll accumulate the loss at each position and average them.

164.text: Process each position one at a time — the model predicts each next character given all previous characters.

165.text: Input token and the target it should predict. The model sees token_id and should output high probability for target_id.

166.text: Run the forward pass, building the entire computation graph. Every Value operation is recorded for backpropagation.

167.text: Convert logits to probabilities. The model's confidence distribution over all 27 possible next characters.

168.text: Negative log probability of the correct answer. If the model assigns probability 1.0 to the right token, loss is 0. Lower probability = higher loss.

170.text: Average loss across all positions in this name. This is the number we minimize — our measure of how wrong the model is.

173.text: One call triggers the entire backward pass through the computation graph. Every parameter's gradient gets computed via the chain rule we built in the Value class.

176.text: Linear warmdown: learning rate starts at 0.01 and linearly decays to 0. Smaller steps near the end help the model settle into a good minimum.

177.text: Iterate over every single parameter in the model for the Adam update.

178.text: Update the running mean of gradients (first moment). beta1 controls how much history to keep vs. how much to trust the current gradient.

179.text: Update the running mean of squared gradients (second moment). This tracks the scale/variance of each parameter's gradients.

180.text: Bias correction for the first moment — compensates for the zero initialization, especially important in early steps.

181.text: Bias correction for the second moment.

182.text: The Adam update: step in the direction of the smoothed gradient, scaled by the inverse of the gradient's historical magnitude. Parameters with large gradients get smaller steps.

183.text: Reset gradient to zero for the next training step. Without this, gradients would accumulate across steps.

188.text: Temperature controls randomness: 0.5 makes the distribution sharper (more conservative), closer to 1.0 gives more variety.

190.text: Generate 20 new names the model has never seen — these come purely from learned patterns.

191.text: Fresh KV caches for generation — no context from training.

192.text: Start with the BOS token — the universal "begin a name" signal.

194.text: Generate one character at a time, up to the max sequence length.

195.text: Feed the current token through the model to get predictions for the next one.

196.text: Apply temperature scaling before softmax. Lower temperature = more confident/predictable outputs.

197.text: Sample from the probability distribution. Unlike greedy decoding (always picking the most likely), sampling introduces variety so not every generation is identical.

198.text: If the model generates BOS, it's signaling "this name is complete."

200.text: Convert the token ID back to its character. The tokenizer goes both ways: chars to IDs for input, IDs to chars for output.

201.text: Print the generated name. These are entirely new names invented by the model, reflecting the statistical patterns it learned from 32K real names.
